sentences.text: When the Judenrat announced we got to go to Biala and from there, we're going to go to Russia to work-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --there was two different stories.
It's going to be a Judenstaat, and then we're going to go to Russia to work.
Then we didn't trust them.
We knew the Germans' tricks.
Then we decided-- the truth, I, myself and another family, we was the first, what we start to run away with the families from the city.
Goldberg.
They took their parents.
No, the parents I took already.
My father was not alive.
This was June the 6th, started.
June the 6th, there was the liquidation for the 3,000 people, where they're supposed to go to Russia to work.
They sent it into Sobibor.
So we start running away.
Anybody what he could take with him, something.
Some of them, they were walking.
Some of them, they go with-- they paid the farmers with horse and wagon to go.
We tried to leave the town, and maybe [INAUDIBLE], because the other towns was from a different district.
The name was [?
Lodzki, ?] Sarnaki, and the other.
So what happened?
The Germans knew already that we disappeared all of us, the most.
We run away.
And then they called into the Judenrat.
They're not going to take any more transports to Russia.
Let them come back.
They're not going to do anything.
Unfortunately, we had to come back, because to be in [?
Lodzki, ?] we didn't have a chance to survive also.
If it was the economical conditions was very bad, you wasn't there.
You didn't have so much place there, where to go to, who to go.
They we came back.
When we came back, was already the house is empty.
Polish people, with the help from the Gestapo.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
--this was a miserable, miserable thing.
Nobody cared anymore for his life, because we knew, how much can you take it?
A few of them got killed coming in by the Germans.
How did they get killed?
The Germans--
The Germans was seeing they're coming.
They catched a couple [INAUDIBLE]. with Jewish people.
They shot them.
That's all.
They knew from where they're coming.
This was they shot them just--
Because they were Jews.
They knew already.
The Polish people told them the whole story.
They knew the whole story, what's going on.
One by the name Leon Busch, a Gestapo, he was a murderer.
He was killing right and left Jews.
And [INAUDIBLE] the other name, I forgot, [INAUDIBLE] tsuris.
Oh, to the municipal, in our town, they sent [INAUDIBLE] from the Biala.
[NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
When he arrived to us, right away, he gave us the first shot.
This was before a year and a half, maybe.
He took away-- we had a car and a horse.
They took away everything.
This was in 1940?
In 1940, right.
Then what happened?
One, I had a friend, [INAUDIBLE].
He was a non-Jew.
He told me who is [INAUDIBLE].
What was the friend's name?
Yusef-- ?]
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [?
Yusef ?] I know.
The second name, I don't remember.
Maybe I'll remind myself later.
So he told me, Louis, this guy, he's an engineer.
He was working in Biala, some little jobs, architectural, in a [INAUDIBLE].
I told him, tell me where he lives.
He told me that this is the place, there, and there, and there, and there, and that's it.
What happened?
Then I took-- it was winter time, cold.
I took-- we had a horse and wagon still that time.
They didn't took away.
And we put in wood, meat, and I went there, and I brought into his house.
To the Ukrainian?
To Ukrainian.
And so that [INAUDIBLE], he was the leader from the municipal.
A mayor.
Then the mother told me, who sent it?
I said-- --I laughed at him.
I went home.
A couple days later, for the weekend, he went to Biala.
Was 18 kilometers.
He came back Sunday night, and he sent it for me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He knew me already, because he gave us-- the first bid we got from him in the city.
He took away this and that, like a [NON-ENGLISH] anti-Semite.
He said, you was in Biala?
I said, yes.
Did you brought in so and so?
Yes.
I'm going to arrest you.
OK, arrest me.
Then he got soft.
And he got-- we came to [NON-ENGLISH].
I was his-- but he respected me.
Then he started to talk to me nicely.
I feel sorry for the Jews, what they're doing to them, and so forth.
But he was a momzer-- the worst.
So what happened?
He told me that he want me to go into the Judenrat.
You're going to help him out.
You're going to do good for your community.
I said, all right.
I called him with a name.
No, I wouldn't go, and I wouldn't do it.
And whenever this, I don't want to be involved.
And this was the story.
A lot of times, the Judenrat had problems-- big problems.
I went, and I [INAUDIBLE] out on the favor for the Judenrat.
He couldn't refuse me, because he figured I am a person that I'm sticking out for the Jews.
In one way, it was anti-Semitic, but in the other way, it felt like I'm going to do for my community.
And this was-- he was there for [INAUDIBLE].
And then they changed it.
But he was pressing very hard, I should go in, in the Judenrat.
And I didn't want it.
I said to myself, I wouldn't do.
I'll take any contribution, anything, any constitution what they're going to take.
I am not going to be.
What happened, that man, he was sent to another city, [INAUDIBLE], about one or two kilometers from our city, by the river Bug.
So what happened?
He sent all Jews from the houses to an Umschlagplatz in the [INAUDIBLE], cleaned up everything, and they sent them back to Russia, that momzer, that same guy, [INAUDIBLE].
Which Umschlagplatz did they send them?
There around the city was a marketplace.
He sent it out to all of them.
Everybody has to go out.
But he didn't kill them.
He just sent them back.
The houses was cleaned out by the Polish people.
Yeah, all the Jewish people, center of town in a market.
What purpose?
[NON-ENGLISH].
This is what I know.
Somebody told me about it.
I was not there, but somebody told me.
Rabkowski,-- ?] a neighbor from the neighboring town, he told me this.
In the beginning, I had-- what do you mean, non-Jews, friendly?
In the beginning.
I had-- It was still, could [INAUDIBLE] live with the police, because we was from before.
I had a good connection.
I made a relationship with the [NON-ENGLISH], with the priest from our town.
Then they get a different [INAUDIBLE] from me, because the priest was-- the priest was [NON-ENGLISH].
Because our doctor [NON-ENGLISH].
And the doctor [INAUDIBLE] without the priest.
I don't remember.
[NON-ENGLISH] I don't remember.
The Polish police, they looked away a lot of things for me, because they knew it from before.
But a lot of times, was they came in, the police, [NON-ENGLISH].
This [INAUDIBLE] prison guard.
I had to go to the [NON-ENGLISH] to get [INAUDIBLE] for that.
They knew already Louis could do something.
And I had a lot of trouble.
I fill one out.
I have to help him, but I [INAUDIBLE].
And this was the rabbi.
I'm mixing up a lot of things, because-- I will mix them up, because--
Right now, we are in 1940.
Yeah.
Let's come to 1942, the liquidation from ghetto.
Because that is for weeks and weeks, you're going to sit with me. you wouldn't go through.
Yom Kippur [INAUDIBLE] 1942, this was already after June the 6th, the liquidation.
There came another story.
We have to go to Biala, and there is going to be a Judenstaat.
And everybody is going to go to the train.
There is going to be prepared for them the cars.
What can I tell you?
This was on a Monday night.
Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday, they got to be cleaned up.
No Jews anymore in the city.
Who gave this order?
The Judenrat announced what they received from Biala, from the main headquarters for the Gestapo.
This come-- they sent a Polish guy, Polish people, with the horse and wagon.
They took out, everybody gone, to the railroad.
And there, they went to Biala.
In Biala, they took in-- wait.
I was the last one to leave my town, me and a few more from my family.
Not the whole family, because my whole family went out a day before.
My sister took the mother.
My mother was not conscious.
Very high blood pressure, and she couldn't walk, but my daughter took her-- my sister took her.
She took her to Biala.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And I was the last one, with a few from my family.
And I had in mind-- when I left, this was already by the evening, by sunset.
I felt so bad, and I cursed at all the world, and with a curse on my [? town. ?] Why you doing?
Why you have to leave it?
Just go on like a prisoner.
And I had in mind, me, and my cousin [INAUDIBLE], and my friend Chaim Goldberg, not to go to Biala.
We'll go about 10 miles, 10 kilometers in the train, and we'll go out in the woods.
But we came to that particular place to go out, but nobody was watching us.
We could go out.
Then I felt terrible.
I said, where are you going to go, Louis?
Look who is going there.
We knew it already, because it is going to disband, because the neighborhood-- the neighborhood, [INAUDIBLE] [?
Lodz, ?] it was cleaned up already with the Jews.
And then I said to myself, where do you go?
You have all of your family.
If they're not going to be alive, what do you need?
You don't [INAUDIBLE] your life.
And I canceled.
I backed out from that plan.
I went into Biala.
I came at night.
No, to Biala.
Oh, yeah, I canceled my plan to go to the woods.
I cannot leave my family.
This is my blood and flesh.
I want to go with them, wherever they'll be.
We arrived Thursday night to Biala, in the evening already.
And the Judenrats from there took us here.
We had an uncle there.
But was no place where to sit in, where to stay.
But we pushed in ourselves, and we came.
Friday morning-- I slept in a foyer outside.
I sleep-- I didn't sleep, even.
But I came.
In the morning, I went out, and I see Jewish people who are digging holes.
The Germans said, this is going to be for the waste from the people, [NON-ENGLISH].
This was not the truth.
The Jewish people dug for themselves.
They killed them, and they throwed them in. 10 o'clock came an order from the Gestapo that Jewish women should give up their jewelry.
Was a tragedy for them to give away wedding rings, for this was from a family life, happy moments in life-- a wedding ring, earrings, this and that.
Everybody went there, and with a broken house, we give it away.
Maybe, maybe, maybe.
In the end, all day was guessing business.
Maybe today, maybe tomorrow.
Maybe it will come and somebody is going to stop the order to kill us.
We knew it already.
We knew the Germans, what kind of momzers [INAUDIBLE].
The worst liars, and the worst bastards, the worst murderers.
Huh?
It will come to it.
So what happened?
I wanted to go away, go out from the town and go in the woods.
But I see my family.
My brother wants to go.
He has no children.
His wife wants to go with me.
And one brother, he has a family with five children-- four children.
He would love to go with me.
How can he leave the wife and the kids?
And they found in me like a leader.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
What can I tell you?
I wanted to go, and go, and go.
All of a sudden, but came 6 o'clock, you cannot go out from the house.
If you should show your face outside, boom.
Then I figure-- Was this a curfew?
A curfew, 6 o'clock.
Wires.
A ghetto, sure.
It was [INAUDIBLE] ghetto.
And then we came in through seven small towns.
The physical was squeezed in.
We came [INAUDIBLE] seven towns.
Was no place where to stay in.
I'll come to it.
So what happened?
Friday night, closed up.
You don't know anything.
All day long was thinking, people, maybe today, maybe tomorrow, maybe somebody is going to stop the tragedy for the Jewish people.
It was already bad. 2 o'clock at night-- in the morning-- started the pogrom of the Jewish people, started knocking on doors and windows and shooting.
And [NON-ENGLISH].
What happened?
My sister, she was the [INAUDIBLE] for my mother.
She said to the family, the mother, we got to live here.
She cannot walk.
She's unconscious.
The [? pressure ?] is high.
Then we left her on the bed with bread, water, and we, everybody, started to go out the Umschlagplatz.
Was a [INAUDIBLE] market.
What can I tell you?
The shooting, the blood was already up to the knees.
Huge blood in the streets.
Some of them, they was asked if they should finish them up, because they was not killed completely.
That particular morning was about 10,000 people from the seven small towns in Biala.
Biala itself had 8,000 Jewish people.
And everybody stayed.
The mothers come in with the kids not dressed.
Was so bad to look at this.
Everybody was shaking.
The mothers couldn't dress the kids because they didn't have lights-- one shoe, one sock, the jacket is opposite.
And the kids, they're pushing to the mothers, because they're shooting.
They're crying.
It's hard to describe.
No pen can [INAUDIBLE].
All of a sudden, you're staying.
And they're coming more, and more, and more, and you're surrounded from Germans.
All kind of Gestapo. sonderkommando, the Luftwaffe, and Polish police, [INAUDIBLE], [?
Ukrainians, ?] you name it.
Finally, the daylight comes up.
Everybody privately was thinking, maybe it's going to come a hand to stop [? that. ?] So what happened?
I finally, in the [INAUDIBLE], I [? found ?] my two brothers.
It's very hard.
It was very hard to find each other.
The beating, the knocking was-- we didn't know, like wolves.
All of them, what I said, just.
SS, the Wehrmacht, and the Gestapo--
They're beating the people, and knocking, and the whole road, maybe about 10 blocks, was already people killed and laying and calling to shoot me, finish me.
Finally, what happened?
They took-- the day is there, and [INAUDIBLE],.
Right away, they took people in the side, should dig holes for themselves.
Me and my two brothers, they took to the Luftwaffe, to the airport.
Outskirts from Biala.
Some of them, they took away in another place.
I didn't know it well.
We didn't know one from the other what's going on.
They took us there, and I didn't know what happened to the wife, the sister, the children, and so forth.
But I go into the airport.
Another Polish [NON-ENGLISH] came in to that direction.
We didn't know what.
They pushed us in to the airport in barracks, and they took us out to work for half a day.
The work was very bad, heavy labor work.
And I see my brother got beaten up already, my older brother.
And--
And then, at night, they took us into the barracks.
No food, no life.
And all night, we was with the Gestapo, with the Ukrainians, [INAUDIBLE], they was murdering us.
They wouldn't let us rest a little bit, or what.
They had a lot.
I don't remember.
It was a lot.
Not all.
Some of them they sent to [INAUDIBLE], near Brest-Litovsk, and some of them other places.
With me, maybe it was about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --150 people, something like that.
One was a rabbi from the next neighbor, neighbor with [INAUDIBLE].
He knew very well me, my father, because he was a [NON-ENGLISH].
Then he said, please don't call me Rabbi.
All right.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I took away.
All right.
Still go.
Yeah, all right.
Ask.
So what happened?
The next day, we find out there were horse and wagons.
They took the rest of the people, women and children, to Miedzyrzec, 26 kilometers.
It was a ghetto.
Miedzyrzec was a ghetto.
From that town, they sent to Treblinka 50,000 people, 10,000 children.
I didn't arrive to Miedzyrzec.
They took me to work.
What happened?
Airport.
The next day, I see it's impossible to work here, to stay here.
Then I went, found a place where there's another group of Jewish people working, maybe a mile away.
Then I went there, and I saw a few people that I know them.
They said, come here.
[INAUDIBLE] will be with us, and we'll be with you.
I went back, and I took my two brothers and a few friends.
We went there to them.
And we went at night in a different barrack from the soldiers.
They didn't-- I was afraid they're going to come to pick us up, but they didn't come.
And the beating was terrible.
No.
I don't know what happened there.
They didn't count, because this was a-- for example, there was there, at night, we had to go through a gate.
They was buildings and buildings and buildings.
We went in the barracks, from the soldiers, from the Polish soldiers.
We were staying there.
The next day, they sent us to work.
Jewish, no Polish.
Jewish people and [INAUDIBLE].
They were staying and laughing from us.
Yeah, Polish people.
Yeah.
So what happened?
We was a few days.
And then--
Oh, go ahead.
Maybe I talk too much.
Maybe I give you too much.
